sentences.text: My name is Morris Rich.
R-I-C-H.
Moshe Rutschaisky.
No.
I didn't-- they didn't use my middle name.
No, I didn't.
Maishke.
Yeah.
Maishke.
Maishke.
Yeah.
Language at birth was actually Yiddish in the house, we were talking.
In public, most Yiddish.
Only in school we were talking Lithuanian and Yiddish.
In April the 20, 1924.
Lithuania.
Kaunas.
Slabodka.
Slabodka?
That's like 10 minutes from Kaunas, only a bridge separated Kaunas from Slabodka.
Slabotka.
Slabotka.
Yeah, it was a suburb, a suburb, yeah.
It's a very small little town by itself.
And Slabodka was very famous, because there was the most biggest yeshivas.
People who want to become rabbis used to come to Slabodka yeshivas, to yeshivas, to learn, you know, to become a rabbi.
That's why Slabodka was famous all over the world.
If you talk to a rabbi in any country, you say you come from Slabodka, they say, ooh, Slabodka.
That's the place who became-- big people came out from Slabodka, big rabbis.
Yeah.
Yeah, I met a lot.
Because a lot of the young people were studying in the Slabodka yeshivas used to rent rooms in my grandmother's house.
And she had all the time like five for six boarders, and they were all what you call yeshiva bocherim.
They were all studying to become rabbis.
These students?
Yes.
They finish their early education, and then they went to become rabbis.
I went to Talmud Torah.
It's called that school, Talmud Torah.
In the same town, and it was more like a religious school.
We learned, you know, Lithuanian, history, geography, and a lot of Hebrew and religious learnings.
Walked, yeah.
Lunch, my mother used to give me in a little package, in a bag, in a little paper bag.
And I used to eat the lunch that my mother used to give it to me to take it to school.
Family?
Well, very devoted parents.
My father, my mother exceptionally devoted.
Because God forbid a child got a little fever, you know?
Like they're calling in this country a 99 and 100, she used to fall in a panic.
[LAUGHS]
No.
My mother was a doctor by herself.
She had already all the remedies.
[LAUGHS] But she learned in all-- only in dangerous situations she called a doctor.
Old fashioned remedies, you know, like certain herbs, you know.
Certain things, you know.
Like now we are-- like they start now in the United States with vitamins.
My mother knew already many years ago, certain things what makes you feel better.
[LAUGHS]
Hodel.
Flink Hodel Flink
My mother was born in 1900.
Lithuania.
Chaim, his first name, Rutschaisky.
Oh, that's-- I'm going to give you a good job now.
It's R-U-T-S-C-H-A-I-S-K-Y.
He was born Lithuania, in Lithuania.
I think my mother told me that she visited her girlfriend, and her girlfriend was married already at that time to a tailor.
And my father knew the tailor.
They were friends with the tailor.
And that's how they met.
Yeah.
My brothers and sisters?
They were the same thing, going to school with me together.
My brother's name is-- my oldest sister's name is Faiga.
My brother's name is Abraham.
And my younger sister's name is Miriam.
And my little brother who got killed in the crematorium with my mother, his name was Solomon.
It was houses like almost like what they build in-- in the Carolinas, from logs, wooden logs, but chopped wooden logs.
And it was a warm-- special build like that to keep warm.
And inside it was wallpaper and beautiful, and it was very comfortable.
Yeah.
We didn't have central air conditioning or central heat.
We had stoves.
And we had in the wintertime to take chopped wood, and keep on heating up the ovens to keep the whole house warm.
But it was a very comfortable, warm house.
And outside the house we had a lot of big fields.
And people grew in the summertime tomatoes, cucumbers.
And all the time when we looked out, we saw green.
Because we lived actually in the outskirts of Slabodka.
We didn't live right in the heart of Slabodka.
We lived already outside.
Because they started to build factories and houses around there.
And we moved there.
Yeah.
We had a yard.
And we had a place, a little to play, even a little football too, soccer.
Yeah.
That's was my main-- my main game was soccer.
Yeah.
We played here, the whole team from Talmud Torah.
And I was-- not to praise myself-- I was very fast.
[LAUGHS] And I played all the time, the outside right.
Yeah.
Sometimes, yeah.
They did.
But I played too much soccer.
My mother used to get angry because sometimes I didn't come on time for dinner.
And she used to say, yeah, he's playing already soccer again, that football.
That's right.
Ping pong we played, a lot of ping pong.
And in the wintertime, we lived not far what is called the [?
Linkuva ?] mountain, big hills.
And we had sleds.
And we used to sled down from the mountains, which is involved in a little danger.
But be did it.
That was our winter sport, to go to that [?
Linkuva ?] mountain, and used to be the sleds to go down.
He was a woodturner.
To make you laugh, he takes a square piece of wood and he makes it round.
But that's not all.
I'm just making a joke.
A woodturner-- a woodturner is who makes parts for furniture, architectural, and industrial, anything what's got to do with shaping out the wood.
And people have to make casting to make metal, or build a home with fancy columns, or use spools for the textile industry.
My father had to supply all these type turnings.
Some of the big textile companies needed very precision spools.
So when the machines run, this spool was not little spools like you'd buy in the store.
It was commercial spools, almost you know, a foot tall, and by 6 inches in diameter.
Build almost, you know, like a tapered bottle.
And these spools had to go very precision.
Otherwise the silk or the cotton tears, so it's got to run very good.
And my father was actually a very precision, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] excellent woodturner.
I used to keep an eye.
He didn't want me to learn-- teach me.
Because woodturning was a poor trade, even the specialties and precision.
But all trades in my country, turnings, woodturning, tailors, shoemakers, all trades were hardly making a good living.
It was--
He worked hard, and he was very good in his trade.
So he made a better living than other woodturners.
Because he was more actually like educated woodturner.
He knew how to read blueprints.
And he was a self-educated person.
He used to read a lot of books.
And he knew how to complete a job according to the blueprint.
And that's why he had more jobs than other woodturners, who did only, you know like, furniture turnings.
My father did a lot of turnings for industrial too.
Before you make a mold, for iron or parts for machinery, it had to be made first in wood, turned.
And then it could be casted.
And that had to be turned according to the blueprint.
And my father was very good.
Of course, I learned.
I used to sneak in, in the shop.
And he used to go to a customer to deliver, and play around with his tools.
But he recognized when he came back, some of the tools was a little chipped.
He says, somebody was already in the shop.
Who was in the shop?
Yeah.
I was a bad boy.
So he didn't want me to go in the shop for one reason.
He says, when you start going to the shop, you'll become a woodturner.
And there is better things than woodturning.
And that's why he didn't like me to go into the shop.
Yeah.
We had all different types.
My father was a well-read person.
He knew everything, what was going on in this world.
Yes.
My father knew that the war is going to come.
Because he didn't only read the Jewish newspapers or Lithuanian newspapers, he even read And according to this, he told all the time my mother, we don't have a chance to hang around Lithuania.
And he knew exactly what is going to be.
But my mother didn't want to believe.
You know, nobody wants to believe in bad things that's going to come out.
But he knew, you know, Hitler's plan.
And when he started to take all the other countries, you know, Czechoslovakia, and he starts with the war in Poland, you know.
But before the war in Poland, he begged my mother.
He says, let's sell the house.
Let's sell the shop.
Let's get out.
Let's go to my family in the United States.
In the United States was my all father's family most, the brothers.
He had two-- he had three sisters in the United States.
And they left many years before the war, lucky.
They left in the right time.
But my mother all the time contradicted him.
She said, oh, Chaim, you're a woodturner.
Look at the big manufacturers, the big names, [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], the big textile.
Actually Jewish tycoon is what you call it in Slabodka.
But they had nice factories.
They are building the factories larger.
And they don't leave Lithuania.
So you are the philosopher?
You are the only smart man?
You want to run?
He says, it's going to be bad, very serious, all standing on an iceberg which is melting under our feet.
And it's going to melt.
And we are all going to be sunk, and drowned, and dead, and murdered.
Do you actually remember these conversations?
Yeah.
I remember all the time.
Because we were kids, and we used to say, yeah, yeah, [?
Papa, ?] let's go to America.
Let's go.
Let's go.
Let's go.
But, you know what happens, you know.
My father was a good husband.
And he didn't want to push too hard.
But when the war broke out, my mother cried bitter tears.
And she said, bitterly crying, look, she says.
Chaim, your father, how right he was.
Look.
Look at exactly-- exactly what he said, exactly the outcome what it is.
Because Hitler had systematically, you know, done what was right in his you know.
All, you know, organized, you know, planned out to kill out all the Jews.
Yeah?
Yes.
First of all, I was going to the Talmud Torah, salient all the time. 40% of the education in Talmud Torah was Hebrew and the Bible, and Tanakh and [?
Chumash, ?] you know, all the Jewish laws, and a little Gemara.
So I knew already, you know, about [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the Jewish, you know, traditions and the Judaism, you know.
And my mother was keeping, of course, her house.
All the people in Slabodka they are kosher.
Yeah.
A lot of non-Jews.
Yeah.
Yeah, We were with some of them, we were very friendly.
And it turned out when the war starts we didn't know we are not friendly.
Because we had very big disappointments.
Because some of our friendly people who were our neighbors, came in right away to look for my father to kill him.
When the war broke out.
They were Lithuanians, you know, gentile people.

And they were preparing for years, if something comes up and they start a war with Poland, and then the Russians in 1940, took Lithuania, and all the Baltic countries, you know, after that pact with Russia.
You see, Germany and Russia made a pact.
And the Russians got certain countries, like a gift, for cutting in, you know with Hitler that peace, so-called pact.
As it was, you know, later nothing.
So the Lithuanians already prepare themselves with rifles and with weapons, digging in, right in their yards.
And I, myself, came in one time to my father.
I says, what is Mazuras, one of our neighbors, Mr.
Mazuras, what is he digging all the time and working in his yard?
And my father says, I don't know.
I hope he doesn't bury weapons there.
Because we had a feeling that he had-- he was like an organizer.
A lot of Lithuanian gentile people used to come to his home.
And the fact is, anytime you know, the telephone was ringing in our house, customers, because we had a telephone, and he didn't have a telephone.
So when people used to call for him, I used to come into his house and said, Mr.
Mazuras, you are called to the telephone.
And we were nice to him all these years.
But not after the-- the war broke out.
After the war broke out, that same man, Mazuras, gave out all [INAUDIBLE] gentile Lithuanians the weapons.
And they got their first ones after six days, when the Germans marched in into Lithuania, did a pogrom in Slabodka.
He didn't kill my mother or our children, but he came to kill my father.
And my father was already killed.

Because he was in the first day, he was going to see if the train is going, and the Lithuanians, already partisans they called themselves, grabbed him right by the train, and they took him away to the Ninth Fort with a lot of other Jewish men, and they killed him later.
Yeah.
May, they marched in May.
It was a big change, of course.
It's-- for the rich people who had the textile factories and all the big enterprises, it was not too happy, because it was right away taken away by the Russians.
And it was made, you know, cooperative factories.
But for the poor people who didn't have anything to lose, the Russians-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were against religion, of course.
So the religious schools were already starting to change.
And it was a change, in general, you know, when the Russians came in.
It was not a happy outcome.
No, by the Russians it was not pogroms.
The Russians just took away from rich people properties, and they wanted to-- everybody to become a Communist.
But not everybody wanted to be a Communist.
When the Germans marched in.
Yeah.
He came into the house.
Yes.
And he says, where is Chaim?
My mother says, Chaim is not here.
We don't know where he is.
We didn't know yet that my father was killed.
He says, you all can be in your house.
Nothing's going to happen to you.
But I must find Chaim.
And he had a hate against my father.
He didn't find Chaim, so he just walked out from the house.
And he says, I'm going to tell my partisans not to touch your house.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But he went, and he killed plenty Jews in other sections.
Oh, yeah.
We knew a lot of families.
It was hard to communicate after the war broke out already.
Because walking from one place to the other, your life was in danger.
Because the Lithuanians, when the war broke out, they were the first killers.
Before even the Germans marched in, the Lithuanians already, they're shooting and killing Jews.
The Lithuanians.
They were preparing already for years in the underground.
